mnemonics.title: COHESION REMINDER
mnemonics.content: COHESIVE MODULE: ONE PURPOSE - THINK 'SINGLE TASK' LIKE A ROBOT DOING ONE JOB

mnemonics.title: COUPLING REMINDER
mnemonics.content: LOOSE COUPLING = INDEPENDENT MODULES - THINK 'STRANDED ON AN ISLAND, EACH SURVIVES ALONE'

mnemonics.title: RECURSION BASE CASE
mnemonics.content: BASE CASE: STOP RECURSION - THINK 'GROUND FLOOR WHERE ELEVATOR STOPS'

mnemonics.title: STORAGE CLASSES
mnemonics.content: AUTO IS AUTOMATIC (LOCAL), REGISTER IS FAST ACCESS, STATIC STAYS, EXTERNAL IS ELSEWHERE

acronyms.expansion: Single Responsibility Principle - A module should have one and only one reason to change
acronyms.usage: Guides module design to ensure each function/module handles one specific task

acronyms.expansion: Application Programming Interface - The set of functions and procedures exposed by a module
acronyms.usage: Defines how other modules can interact with a given module

acronyms.expansion: Input-Processing-Output - A basic module design pattern
acronyms.usage: Each function should clearly define what it takes in, what it does, and what it returns

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box labeled with function name. Show arrows entering (parameters/inputs) and arrows leaving (return value/outputs). The inside is hidden—this represents information hiding.
Draw a tree with main() at top, branching to subordinate functions below. Each branch represents a module calling sub-modules. This shows the top-down decomposition structure.
Draw stacked boxes growing downward. Each box represents a recursive call. The base case is the bottom box. As you unwind, each box closes and returns a value upward.
Draw two scenarios: (1) COPY scenario - a photocopy of document being edited, original unchanged; (2) POINTER scenario - giving someone the original document folder to modify directly.

analogies.analogy: Like kitchen appliances - each has a specific purpose (blender, toaster, oven). You don't need one appliance to do everything, and you can replace one without affecting others.
Like a name tag at a party - it tells you WHO someone is before you actually meet and talk to them (before the function definition).
Like looking into two mirrors facing each other - you see infinite reflections, each smaller reflection containing the next, until it becomes too small to see (base case).
Like lego blocks - each piece connects easily but can be rearranged independently. High coupling would be like gluing pieces together - hard to separate.
Like a desk that keeps your work between sessions - even when you leave the room (function ends), your papers are still there when you return.
